chunk_summary: Guidelines for diagnosing dysglycemia in children focus on appropriate tests, diagnostic cutoffs, and risk-based screening after age 10 or pubertal onset, emphasizing the avoidance of overdiagnosis.
learning_objectives: Understand the recommended diagnostic tests and cutoffs for dysglycemia in children.
Identify risk factors and appropriate timing for dysglycemia screening in pediatric populations.
Recognize the limitations of fasting insulin and HOMA-IR in dysglycemia screening.
Apply risk-based screening strategies for children with obesity or other risk factors.
key_facts: Prediabetes diagnostic cutoffs: fasting glucose 100–125 mg/dL, 2-hour OGTT 140–199 mg/dL, HbA1c 5.7–6.4%.
Diabetes diagnostic cutoffs: fasting glucose ≥126 mg/dL, 2-hour OGTT ≥200 mg/dL, HbA1c ≥6.5% (confirmation required unless unequivocal hyperglycemia).
Glucose load for OGTT in children: 1.75 g/kg (maximum 75 g).
Screening recommended after age 10 or pubertal onset in overweight/obese children with additional risk factors.
OGTT is reserved for high-risk phenotypes (e.g., severe obesity, MASLD) or discordant/borderline results.
Fasting insulin and HOMA-IR are not recommended for screening due to variability.
reference_quote: The 2023 AAP obesity guideline recommends evaluating abnormal glucose metabolism using fasting plasma glucose, 2-hour plasma glucose after an oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT), or HbA1c.
document_key_topics: Diagnostic tests and cutoffs for dysglycemia in children
Risk-based screening for dysglycemia in pediatric populations
Limitations of fasting insulin and HOMA-IR in screening
